What flows.evidence: Trigger Engine → Case Manager: event-driven, idempotent, with reconciliation against the client master nightly.
Case Manager → Audit Ledger: every state transition, hash-chained, retained 10 years (matches AMLD record-keeping).
Outreach Service → Mobile App: uses the same secure-message rail as transactional approvals; supports step-up auth.
Case Manager → Core Banking: writes back nextReviewDate, riskRating, and any restrictions via the existing client-master update API.

Description.evidence: No system of record. Evidence lives in four places (shared drive, email, SharePoint, core banking). Reconciliation is manual. (§2 Why it hurts, p.7)

Description.evidence: Audit fragility. Reconstructing why a review was approved requires pulling four artefacts, none of which are timestamped consistently. (§2 Why it hurts, p.7)

Impact.evidence: Two regulatory findings (BaFin §44 KWG inspection, Sep 2025; internal audit report IA-2025-117) have been raised against control execution, ageing and evidence completeness. (Executive Summary, p.5); G-02: Evidence scattered across 4 systems — Critical — Q1 2027. (§9 Gap Log, p.16)

Inputs.evidence: Client master, last-review date, risk rating, event bus (sanctions, adverse media, transaction-monitoring, BO change).

Outputs.evidence: Output. ReviewDue event with reason code.

What happens.evidence: Triggers are deterministic and logged. A review fires when any of: The client's review cadence expires (Low 5y / Med 3y / High 1y). An event-based trigger fires: a sanctions/PEP hit, an adverse-media match above threshold, a transaction-monitoring case closure with recommendation RECHECK_KYC, or a confirmed beneficial-owner change. A ReviewDue event never silently expires. If it is not picked up within 72 hours, the Case Manager auto-opens the case and notifies the queue owner.

Why it matters.evidence: Reviews are triggered automatically by a central trigger engine — never by an RM remembering a date.

Inputs.evidence: When the case opens it is pre-populated with everything the bank already knows: Identity documents and their expiry (Document Vault). Address on file (Client Master) plus the last three statement-delivery bounce-backs. Source-of-funds signal from the last 12 months of transactions (Transaction Datamart). Beneficial-owner graph (Entity Resolution Service). Latest sanctions / PEP / adverse-media screens (Screening Service). Last KYC decision and its evidence pack.

Outputs.evidence: The case is assigned a completeness score (0–100) and an STP eligibility flag.

What happens.evidence: When the case opens it is pre-populated with everything the bank already knows. The case is assigned a completeness score (0–100) and an STP eligibility flag.

Inputs.evidence: Owner. STP Decision Engine. Eligibility. Low or Medium risk and completeness ≥ 92 and no open screening hit and no event-based trigger and product mix unchanged.

Outputs.evidence: If eligible, the engine: Refreshes the client's risk rating (re-running the rating model). Writes the new nextReviewDate to the client master. Posts the decision and the full evidence snapshot to the Audit Ledger. Notifies the RM (FYI) — no client contact.

What happens.evidence: Owner. STP Decision Engine. Eligibility. Low or Medium risk and completeness ≥ 92 and no open screening hit and no event-based trigger and product mix unchanged. If ineligible, the case is routed to Reviewer Triage with the reason spelt out (e.g. 'ID document expires in < 90 days; address bounce-back twice in 6 months').

Why it matters.evidence: ~62 % of Low- and Medium-risk reviews complete straight-through using authoritative data already held by the bank (no client contact).

Inputs.evidence: Owner. Client Outreach Service. Channel. Mobile app (primary), secure message (secondary), RM-mediated for Private Banking. The system computes the minimal data delta — what is missing or stale — and asks for only that. No re-collection of documents the bank holds and can still verify.

What happens.evidence: The system computes the minimal data delta — what is missing or stale — and asks for only that. No re-collection of documents the bank holds and can still verify. Outreach uses a single thread, has a hard 30-day timeout, and falls back to RM-mediated outreach if the client does not respond on the digital channel.

Why it matters.evidence: Targeted outreach based on the minimal data delta removes the friction of re-uploading documents the bank already holds, reducing outreach rate from 91% to a target of 38% by Year 1.

Inputs.evidence: Owner. Financial Crime Operations Analyst (FCO Analyst). SLA. 5 working days from case ready-for-review. The analyst sees a single screen with: The completeness score and the explicit reason for non-STP. The full evidence pack, with provenance — every fact links to its source record. The risk model's rating with feature contributions. Open screening hits with the analyst's tooling to clear or escalate.

Outputs.evidence: Decision options: Approve. Recorded with rationale. Approve with conditions. E.g. monitoring tier raised, debit cap applied, product restriction. Refer to EDD. Triggers the Enhanced Due Diligence subprocess. Recommend exit. Sent to FCO sign-off (Step 6).

Inputs.evidence: Mandatory for: High-risk clients, PEPs, recommend-exit decisions, approve-with-conditions where the condition restricts a regulated product, and a random 5 % QA sample of STP and analyst approvals.

What happens.evidence: Owner. Financial Crime Officer (FCO) — 4-eyes principle. Mandatory for: High-risk clients, PEPs, recommend-exit decisions, approve-with-conditions where the condition restricts a regulated product, and a random 5 % QA sample of STP and analyst approvals. Sign-off is recorded against the FCO's identity (SSO) and is immutable.

Why it matters.evidence: Opaque escalation. Whether a case escalates to FCO depends on the analyst on duty, not a written rule.

Outputs.evidence: On approval: Risk rating refreshed. Next review date written to the client master. Outreach threads archived to the case. Audit Ledger entry sealed. RM notified (informational). On exit / restriction: Client Exit workflow opened, or product / channel restrictions applied via the Restrictions Service. Customer Communications notified to draft the regulatory-compliant exit letter.

What happens.evidence: Owner. Case Manager (system). On approval: Risk rating refreshed. Next review date written to the client master. Outreach threads archived to the case. Audit Ledger entry sealed. RM notified (informational). On exit / restriction: Client Exit workflow opened, or product / channel restrictions applied via the Restrictions Service. Customer Communications notified to draft the regulatory-compliant exit letter.

Why it matters.evidence: End — happy path: Reviewer approval recorded → Risk rating refreshed, next-review date written back to client master. End — unhappy path: Senior FCO sign-off on exit / restriction → Client exit workflow opened or restrictions applied (debit block, product caps).

Rationale.evidence: The root cause of overdue reviews is incentive misalignment, not tooling. RMs are measured on AUM and revenue; periodic reviews are a cost. Moving accountability to FCO and reducing RM involvement to a consultation role removes the incentive conflict.

The decision.evidence: Periodic KYC Review is owned by Financial Crime Operations. The RM owns the client relationship but is not accountable for review execution.

Rationale.evidence: Case management is a commodity; integration depth and configurability matter more than uniqueness. The trigger / decision / audit layer is our competitive and regulatory differentiator and must not be a black box to FCO and Internal Audit.

The decision.evidence: Buy a vendor case-management platform (Fenergo or ComplyAdvantage Mesh). Build the Trigger Engine, the STP Decision Engine, and the Audit Ledger in-house.

Options considered.evidence: Let the model decide; cap at 50 %.

Rationale.evidence: 70 % balances cost-out against the supervisory expectation that human review remains a material control. The cap is removable when challenger model validation has run for ≥ 12 months.

The decision.evidence: Hard cap STP share at 70 % of the eligible book, even if the model would route more.

Rationale.evidence: Mobile app outreach has a 7-day median completion in the pilot vs. 21 days for email; RM-mediated remains essential for PB tone-of-voice and for clients without the app (~9 % of the book).

The decision.evidence: All non-PB outreach defaults to the mobile app, with RM-mediated as a 30-day fall-back. PB stays RM-mediated.

Options considered.evidence: Trust SharePoint logs; trust the case manager's internal log.

Rationale.evidence: Closes the BaFin §44 finding decisively. The hash chain makes silent tampering detectable; the external checkpoint defends against insider edits. Cost is low (≤ CHF 60k / year).

The decision.evidence: A single audit ledger for all KYC decisions, append-only, with a Merkle hash chain checkpointed to an external timestamping authority weekly.

Rationale.evidence: Group internal audit has flagged inconsistency between booking centres twice. A single framework is defensible to the lead supervisor (FINMA as group regulator).

The decision.evidence: One SLA framework with risk-rating-driven tiers, applied identically across booking centres.
